questions.stem: How does the lecture define 'software change'?

options.text: The process of adding new functionality to existing code
The process of removing all defects from a released product
The process of porting code to a new programming language
The process of writing a brand-new system from scratch

questions.explanation: Software change is the process of adding new functionality to existing code, the foundation of evolution and servicing. Writing from scratch is green-field development; removing all defects is only corrective work; porting languages is one narrow adaptive case, not the definition. (SoftwareChange p.2)

questions.stem: Software change is named the foundation of which two activities?
questions.explanation: The deck calls software change the foundation of software evolution and servicing. Compilation/linking are build steps; testing/deployment are activities but not what change founds; requirements/design are phases inside a change. (SoftwareChange p.2)

questions.stem: What distinguishes software change from green-field development?

options.text: Software change never involves writing any new code
Software change excludes any form of testing
Software change always operates on existing code
Software change is performed only by the original authors

questions.explanation: Change always operates on existing code, the central premise of maintenance; green-field starts from nothing. It does write new code (it adds functionality), can be done by any maintainer, and includes verification. (SoftwareChange p.2, p.5)

questions.stem: In the course's vocabulary, what is a single software change (SC)?

options.text: A batch of unrelated bug fixes released together
A single line edit committed to version control
The entire lifetime of a software product
One disciplined trip through the eight-phase change process

questions.explanation: A single SC is one disciplined pass through the phased process; evolution and servicing accumulate many such changes. It is not a lone line edit, not the whole product lifetime (that is evolution), and not an unstructured batch. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: According to Lientz & Swanson, which maintenance category accounts for roughly 50% of effort?
questions.explanation: Perfective maintenance (improving/extending functionality, e.g. credit-card support) dominates at about 50%. Adaptive is about 25%, corrective about 21%, preventive about 4%. (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: Which Lientz & Swanson category covers adapting software to a changed environment, exemplified by the Y2K fixes?
questions.explanation: Adaptive maintenance adapts software to a changed environment; the deck's example is the Y2K date fixes (~25%). Perfective extends functionality, corrective fixes bugs, preventive improves structure. (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: In the Lientz & Swanson taxonomy, fixing defects or bugs is classified as which kind of maintenance?
questions.explanation: Corrective maintenance fixes defects/bugs (~21%). Adaptive responds to environment change, perfective extends functionality, preventive improves structure to forestall future problems. (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: Improving code structure to forestall future problems, the smallest Lientz & Swanson slice (~4%), is which category?
questions.explanation: Preventive maintenance improves code structure to prevent future issues (~4%) and is closely related to refactoring and reducing technical debt. Corrective fixes existing bugs, adaptive handles environment change, perfective adds capability. (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: Adding credit-card support to an application is the deck's example of which maintenance category?
questions.explanation: Adding credit-card support extends functionality for users, the textbook example of perfective maintenance. It is not driven by an environment change (adaptive), a defect (corrective), or structural cleanup (preventive). (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: Why does the dominance of perfective maintenance matter for the design of a change process?

options.text: It shows corrective maintenance can be ignored entirely
It motivates a process tuned for adding functionality to existing code rather than just patching bugs
It proves that most code never needs to be tested
It means refactoring should never be performed

questions.explanation: Because perfective work dominates, the canonical process is tuned for adding functionality to existing code, not merely patching. Testing is still required, corrective work still happens (~21%), and refactoring is part of pre/postfactoring. (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: Classified by impact on functionality, what is an 'incremental' change?
questions.explanation: Incremental change adds new functionality. Removing obsolete functionality is contraction, swapping functionality is replacement, restructuring without behavior change is refactoring. (SoftwareChange p.4)

questions.stem: Which impact-on-functionality category means removing obsolete functionality?
questions.explanation: Contraction removes obsolete functionality. Incremental adds new functionality, replacement swaps one for another, refactoring changes structure only. (SoftwareChange p.4)

questions.stem: Among the four impacts on functionality, which one is defined as changing software structure WITHOUT changing behavior?
questions.explanation: Refactoring changes structure without changing behavior, a definition repeated throughout the course. Incremental adds, contraction removes, and replacement substitutes functionality, all of which alter behavior. (SoftwareChange p.4)

questions.stem: Swapping out an existing feature for a different implementation of the same capability is which impact-on-functionality type?
questions.explanation: Replacement replaces existing functionality. Contraction removes it outright, incremental adds brand-new functionality, refactoring leaves behavior unchanged. (SoftwareChange p.4)

questions.stem: The guide notes a conceptual link between a Lientz & Swanson category (the why) and an impact-on-functionality type (the how). Which pairing is it?

options.text: Perfective maintenance is typically realized as refactoring
Adaptive maintenance is typically realized as replacement
Preventive maintenance is typically realized as refactoring
Corrective maintenance is typically realized as contraction

questions.explanation: Preventive maintenance (the why) is typically realized as refactoring (the how), both about improving structure. Corrective is not contraction, adaptive is not inherently replacement, and perfective adds functionality (incremental), not behavior-preserving refactoring. (SoftwareChange p.3-4)

questions.stem: How many distinct categories does the Lientz & Swanson taxonomy classify maintenance into?
questions.explanation: Lientz & Swanson defines four categories: perfective, adaptive, corrective, preventive. Three and two undercount; five overcounts (the four impacts-on-functionality are a separate taxonomy). (SoftwareChange p.3)

questions.stem: On what basis does the Lientz & Swanson taxonomy classify a change?

options.text: How many lines of code it touches

questions.explanation: Lientz & Swanson classifies by why the change is done (perfective/adaptive/corrective/preventive). The how-it-affects-functionality axis (incremental/contraction/replacement/refactoring) is the separate impact taxonomy; author and LOC are not classification bases. (SoftwareChange p.3-4)

questions.stem: A team restructures a module's class hierarchy to make an upcoming feature easier, with no user-visible behavior change. By the two L02 taxonomies, how is this best described?

options.text: Perfective maintenance realized as incremental change

questions.explanation: Improving structure to ease future work is preventive (why), and changing structure without changing behavior is refactoring (how). It adds no user feature (so not perfective/incremental), fixes no bug (not corrective), and removes nothing (not contraction). (SoftwareChange p.3-4)

questions.stem: The full canonical change cycle (Initiation through Conclusion with Verification) is described in the deck as what?

options.text: A single phase of agile development
A legacy process no longer used
The main topic of this course

questions.explanation: The deck states the full change cycle is the main topic of this course. It is central, not optional, not legacy, and is an eight-phase process rather than one agile phase. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: Which sequence correctly orders the implementation-group phases of the canonical change process?
questions.explanation: The implementation group runs Prefactoring (before), Actualization (the change), then Postfactoring (after). The other orders misplace the before/after refactoring around the actual change. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: Which option lists the canonical change phases in correct top-to-bottom order?

options.text: Initiation, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring, Conclusion
Concept Location, Initiation, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, Actualization, Conclusion, Postfactoring
Initiation, Impact Analysis, Concept Location, Actualization, Prefactoring, Postfactoring, Conclusion
Initiation, Concept Location, Prefactoring, Impact Analysis, Actualization, Postfactoring, Conclusion

questions.explanation: The canonical order is Initiation, Concept Location, Impact Analysis, Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring, Conclusion (Verification spans the implementation phases). The distractors swap Concept Location with Impact Analysis, reorder Initiation, or move Prefactoring ahead of Impact Analysis. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: Which two phases are grouped as 'interactions with the world'?
questions.explanation: Initiation and Conclusion are the world-interaction phases (a request comes in; the result is committed and released). Concept Location and Impact Analysis are SC design; Prefactoring/Actualization/Postfactoring are SC implementation. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: Which two phases form the 'SC design' group?
questions.explanation: Concept Location and Impact Analysis are the SC design phases. Initiation/Conclusion are world interactions; Prefactoring/Actualization/Postfactoring are implementation. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: Which three phases make up the 'SC implementation' group?
questions.explanation: SC implementation is Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring. Concept Location and Impact Analysis are design; Initiation and Conclusion are world interactions; Verification spans phases rather than belonging to one group. (SoftwareChange p.5)

questions.stem: How does Verification fit into the phased change model?

options.text: It runs vertically alongside Prefactoring through Conclusion
It is a single final phase after Conclusion

questions.explanation: Verification runs vertically alongside the implementation phases (Prefactoring through Conclusion), guaranteeing correctness throughout. It is not a single final step, is not confined to Initiation, and does not replace Postfactoring. (SoftwareChange p.5, p.13)

questions.stem: Which pair of phases does Lecture 2 cover in depth, previewing the rest?
questions.explanation: L02 covers Initiation and Concept Location in depth (phases 1-2) and previews phases 3-8. The other pairs are among the previewed-only phases. (SoftwareChange p.5, guide Overview)

questions.stem: With which phase does a software change start?

options.text: A change request in the Initiation phase

questions.explanation: SC starts with a change request, captured in Initiation. Concept Location follows once a request exists; Actualization and Conclusion come much later. (SoftwareChange p.6, ChangeInitiation p.2)

questions.stem: In the phased model, Concept Location sits between which two phases?
questions.explanation: Concept Location follows Initiation and feeds Impact Analysis: concepts are extracted from the request, located in code, and the located classes seed Impact Analysis. The other pairs are later in the spine. (SoftwareChange p.8)

questions.stem: What does the Impact Analysis phase determine?

options.text: The change strategy and the impact set of affected classes
The natural-language wording of the user story
The color scheme of the GUI

questions.explanation: Impact Analysis determines the change strategy and the impact set of affected classes. Wording the user story is Initiation; branch choice is Conclusion/version control; GUI styling is unrelated. (SoftwareChange p.9)

questions.stem: How is the initial impact set formed at the start of Impact Analysis?

options.text: From a random sample of supplier modules
From the classes identified during Concept Location
From the classes touched in the previous release
From every class in the entire system

questions.explanation: The classes found in Concept Location make up the initial impact set; dependency analysis then grows it. It is not every class, not last release's classes, and not a random sample. (SoftwareChange p.9)

questions.stem: After the initial impact set is formed, how does Impact Analysis grow it?

options.text: By deleting classes that compile cleanly
By analyzing class dependencies and adding impacted classes
By committing the change to version control

questions.explanation: Impact Analysis analyzes class dependencies and adds impacted classes to the set. It does not delete by compile status, renaming is not part of growing the set, and committing belongs to Conclusion. (SoftwareChange p.9)

questions.stem: In the deck's figure for the design phases, how is the impact set visualized?

options.text: A flat list of file names
A pie chart of maintenance categories
A concept at the centre with the impact set radiating outward through the software

questions.explanation: The figure shows a concept at the centre with the impact set radiating outward through the software. The other depictions belong to different slides or are not used. (SoftwareChange p.7)

questions.stem: How does the deck define prefactoring?

options.text: Opportunistic refactoring that localizes (minimizes) the impact of the change
Refactoring done after the change to remove anti-patterns
Committing the change to version control

questions.explanation: Prefactoring is opportunistic refactoring that localizes/minimizes the impact of SC, done before the change. Refactoring after to remove anti-patterns is postfactoring; writing the feature is actualization; committing is conclusion. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: Which two named Fowler refactorings does the deck give as prefactoring examples?

options.text: Rename Variable and Pull Up Method

questions.explanation: The deck names Extract Class (gather fields/methods into a new component) and Extract Superclass (create a new abstract class) as prefactoring examples. The others are real Fowler refactorings but not the ones cited here. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: The Extract Class prefactoring refactoring does what?

options.text: Gathers fields, methods, and code snippets into a new component class

questions.explanation: Extract Class gathers fields, methods, and code snippets into a new component class. Creating a new abstract class is Extract Superclass; deleting and merging are different operations. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: Why is prefactoring performed BEFORE the new code is written?

options.text: To prioritize the change request in the backlog
To remove anti-patterns the change has already introduced
To verify the change against test cases
To make the upcoming change small and local, minimizing its impact

questions.explanation: Prefactoring happens before the change to localize and minimize impact, making the change small and local. Removing introduced anti-patterns is postfactoring (after); verification and backlog prioritization are different phases. (SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: What does the Actualization phase do?

options.text: Creates new code, plugs it into the old code, and visits neighboring classes to update them
Commits the baseline to version control
Extracts concepts from the change request

questions.explanation: Actualization creates the new code, plugs it into existing code, and updates neighboring classes. Concept extraction is Concept Location, committing is Conclusion, prioritizing is Initiation. (SoftwareChange p.11)

questions.stem: During Actualization, what is 'change propagation'?

options.text: Copying the change to every branch
Updating neighboring classes so the system stays consistent
Reverting the change if a test fails

questions.explanation: Change propagation is the deliberate act of updating neighbors to keep the system consistent. The uncontrolled cascade is the ripple effect; the other options are unrelated to the term. (SoftwareChange p.11)

questions.stem: How does the 'ripple effect' differ from 'change propagation' in Actualization?

options.text: The ripple effect happens only during Initiation
They are two names for the same act of committing code
The ripple effect is changes cascading outward through dependencies; change propagation is the deliberate updating of neighbors to stay consistent
Change propagation removes anti-patterns introduced by the change

questions.explanation: The ripple effect is the cascade of changes outward through dependencies; change propagation is the maintainer's act of updating neighbors to keep the system consistent. They are distinct, occur in Actualization, and neither is committing or anti-pattern removal. (SoftwareChange p.11)

questions.stem: What does the Postfactoring phase eliminate?

options.text: Supplier modules from the dependency graph
Anti-patterns that the change may have introduced

questions.explanation: Postfactoring eliminates anti-patterns the change may have introduced (e.g. long method, bloated class). It does not remove the request, suppliers, or the backlog. (SoftwareChange p.12)

questions.stem: Which two anti-patterns does the deck name as targets of postfactoring?
questions.explanation: The deck names long method and bloated class as anti-patterns postfactoring cleans up after a change. The others are real smells but not the cited examples. (SoftwareChange p.12)

questions.stem: Postfactoring is best described as the mirror of which phase?

options.text: Prefactoring, but cleaning up after the change instead of before
Actualization, but reverting code instead of adding it

questions.explanation: Postfactoring mirrors prefactoring: prefactoring refactors before to minimize impact, postfactoring refactors after to remove introduced anti-patterns. It is not reverting, skipping tests, or a variant of Conclusion. (SoftwareChange p.10, p.12)

questions.stem: According to the deck, what does Verification comprise?
questions.explanation: Verification comprises testing (functional, unit, structural) and walkthroughs. It is broader than manual reading alone, broader than unit tests alone, and is not the build step. (SoftwareChange p.13)

questions.stem: Which three kinds of testing does the deck list under Verification?
questions.explanation: The deck lists functional, unit, and structural testing under Verification. The other triplets are real testing terms but not the ones enumerated on this slide. (SoftwareChange p.13)

questions.stem: What does the Conclusion phase do?

options.text: Commits the finished code to version control, builds the new baseline, and prepares for the next change
Extracts concepts from the change request

questions.explanation: Conclusion commits to version control, builds the new baseline, and prepares for the next change, closing the loop to Initiation. Concept extraction, prefactoring, and user-story writing are earlier phases. (SoftwareChange p.14)

questions.stem: The Conclusion phase is the lecture's explicit tie-in to which later course topics?

options.text: Version Control / Git and continuous integration

questions.explanation: Conclusion (commit, new baseline, prepare for next change) ties into Version Control / Git and CI. Concept location, design patterns, and user stories are introduced in other parts of L02. (SoftwareChange p.14)

questions.stem: A maintainer has just finished writing and integrating new feature code and notices a method has become overly long. Which phase addresses this, and what is it called?
questions.explanation: Cleaning up a long method introduced by the change is postfactoring. Prefactoring is before the change; actualization is writing/plugging in the code; verification tests correctness but does not remove the smell. (SoftwareChange p.12)

questions.stem: Across the whole spine, which statement about phase grouping AND Verification is fully correct?

options.text: All eight items are implementation phases and Verification is the first of them
Initiation and Conclusion are implementation phases, and Verification runs only at Conclusion
Concept Location and Impact Analysis are world interactions, and Verification spans only Initiation

Initiation/Conclusion are world interactions, Concept Location/Impact Analysis are design, Prefactoring/Actualization/Postfactoring are implementation, and Verification spans the implementation phases

questions.explanation: The correct mapping is world interactions (Initiation, Conclusion), design (Concept Location, Impact Analysis), implementation (Prefactoring, Actualization, Postfactoring), with Verification spanning the implementation phases through Conclusion. The distractors mislabel the groups or confine Verification incorrectly. (SoftwareChange p.5, p.13)

questions.stem: Besides capturing requirements, what else does the Initiation phase include?
questions.explanation: Initiation captures requirements and prioritizes change requests. Cloning prototypes is the Prototype pattern, backtracking is in dependency search, and building the baseline is Conclusion. (ChangeInitiation p.2)

questions.stem: According to the Change Initiation deck, a requirement triggering a change may be which of the following?

options.text: A software bug, an enhancement, or an improvement

questions.explanation: The deck says the requirement may be a software bug, an enhancement, or an improvement. Limiting it to only a bug, a mockup, or a failed test misses the full range. (ChangeInitiation p.2)

questions.stem: A user reports a software bug. Which Lientz & Swanson category does this trigger map to?
questions.explanation: A user reporting a bug triggers corrective maintenance. A user asking for an enhancement would be perfective; environment change drives adaptive; structural cleanup is preventive. (ChangeInitiation p.3)

questions.stem: A user asks for an enhancement to the software. Which maintenance category does this trigger map to?
questions.explanation: An enhancement request maps to perfective maintenance (extending functionality). A bug report is corrective, structural cleanup is preventive, environment adaptation is adaptive. (ChangeInitiation p.3)

questions.stem: A manager wants the product to match a competitor's functionality. Which trigger/category description fits best?

options.text: A business-driven, perfective change initiated by a manager stakeholder
A corrective change initiated by a user reporting a bug
An adaptive change forced by a new operating environment
A preventive change initiated by a programmer improving structure

questions.explanation: A manager wanting competitor parity is a business-driven perfective trigger. It is not a bug (corrective), not a programmer's structural improvement (preventive), and not environment-forced (adaptive). (ChangeInitiation p.3)

questions.stem: The Change Initiation deck shows a sticky-note mnemonic for capturing a request. What is it?
questions.explanation: The deck's mnemonic is 'As a [who], I want [what], because [why]', the user-story form. Given/when/then is BDD (later lecture); input/process/output and the sequencing words are unrelated. (ChangeInitiation p.3)

questions.stem: Which forms can a change request take, according to the deck?

options.text: A sentence or paragraph, a bug report, or a user story

questions.explanation: A change request can be a sentence/paragraph, a bug report, or a user story (the last preferred for new functionality). UML diagrams and commit messages are not the listed request forms. (ChangeInitiation p.4)

questions.stem: Which change-request form does the deck prefer for new functionality?
questions.explanation: The user story is preferred for new functionality. A bug report suits defects, a free-form paragraph is less structured, and a stack trace is debugging output, not a request form. (ChangeInitiation p.4)

questions.stem: What physical size constraint does the deck place on a user story?

options.text: It must fit on a 3-by-5-inch card
It must fit on a single A4 page
It must fit in one Git commit

questions.explanation: A user story must fit on a 3-by-5-inch card, a deliberate device to keep each change small. A4 page, 280 characters, and one commit are not the stated rule. (ChangeInitiation p.4)

questions.stem: What is the deck's rule when a new piece of functionality will not fit on a single user-story card?

options.text: Skip the user story and code directly
Divide it into several user stories
Compress the font until it fits

questions.explanation: If new functionality cannot fit on the card, it must be divided into several user stories. Shrinking text, silently dropping requirements, or skipping the story defeat the size rule's purpose of keeping changes small. (ChangeInitiation p.4)

questions.stem: Why does the deck impose the 3-by-5-inch card size on user stories?

options.text: To match the size of a standard index in a library
To enforce a fixed font size
To limit complexity and the potential for misunderstanding, keeping each change small enough to flow through one pass of the process

questions.explanation: The card-size constraint limits complexity and misunderstanding, keeping each change small enough for one clean pass of the change process. The other reasons are incidental and not the stated rationale. (ChangeInitiation p.4)

questions.stem: How does the deck define a user story?

options.text: A test case written in Gherkin
A formal specification with pre- and post-conditions
A short, simple description of a capability written from the perspective of the person who desires it

questions.explanation: A user story is a short, simple description of a capability from the desirer's perspective. Formal pre/post-conditions, Gherkin tests, and UML diagrams are different artifacts. (ChangeInitiation p.5)

questions.stem: Which is the canonical user-story template from the deck?

options.text: As a [user type], I want [some goal] so that [some reason]

questions.explanation: The canonical template is 'As a [user type], I want [some goal] so that [some reason]'. Given/when/then is BDD; if/then/else is code; subject/verb/object is a grammar pattern. (ChangeInitiation p.5)

questions.stem: Which is a well-formed user story matching the deck's template?

options.text: . When a user logs in, the system shows information
As a valid user, I want to access the system so that I can review my information
System: provide access; User: review info
Access the system to review information

questions.explanation: The deck's worked example follows 'As a [user type], I want [goal] so that [reason]'. The other options drop the role and rationale or use a non-story format. (ChangeInitiation p.6)

questions.stem: In the deck's administrator example, the story reads 'As an administrator, I want to restrict access to the system to valid users so that ...'. How does it end?

options.text: ...so that I can ensure we protect user information

questions.explanation: The administrator story ends '...so that I can ensure we protect user information'. Performance, server count, and UI styling are not the stated reason. (ChangeInitiation p.6)

questions.stem: Which clause of a user story captures the business justification for the capability?
questions.explanation: The 'so that [reason]' clause captures the justification/value. 'As a [user type]' names the role, 'I want [goal]' names the desired capability, and the title is just a label. (ChangeInitiation p.5)

questions.stem: What is the Product Backlog?

options.text: A prioritized wish list of requirements
A record of past Git commits
A list of resolved bugs only

questions.explanation: The Product Backlog is a prioritized wish list of requirements that stakeholders add/delete/modify over time. It is not a closed-bug log, a commit history, or a dependency diagram. (ChangeInitiation p.7)

questions.stem: Which prioritization tiers does the deck use for the Product Backlog?

options.text: Must have / Nice to have / Won't have

questions.explanation: The deck uses Must have / Nice to have / Won't have, a MoSCoW-style stacking. Critical/Major/Minor, Now/Next/Later, and P0/P1/P2 are other schemes not used here. (ChangeInitiation p.7)

questions.stem: Why does the Product Backlog change over time, per the deck?

options.text: It must be reset after every release
Compilers reorder it during the build
Additional knowledge is acquired by users and additional clarification is needed by developers
The version-control system rewrites it automatically

questions.explanation: The backlog evolves because users acquire additional knowledge and developers need additional clarification. It is not auto-rewritten by tooling, compilers, or a forced post-release reset. (ChangeInitiation p.7)

questions.stem: In the end-to-end Change Initiation picture, what role does the Product Backlog play?

options.text: It records the impact set after Impact Analysis
It is where the new code is plugged into existing code
It is the container that holds the Change Request between Initiation and the actual Change

questions.explanation: In the flow Stakeholders -> Initiation -> Change Request (in the Backlog) -> Change, the backlog holds the change request until the change is made. It does not store binaries, perform code integration, or record the impact set. (ChangeInitiation p.8)

questions.stem: Which sequence matches the deck's end-to-end change-initiation flow?

options.text: Stakeholders -> Change Initiation -> Change Request (in Backlog) -> Change -> New Code plugged into Existing Code to reach Desired Code
New Code -> Existing Code -> Change Request -> Stakeholders -> Backlog
Existing Code -> Stakeholders -> Backlog -> Desired Code -> Change
Change -> Change Request -> Stakeholders -> New Code -> Backlog

questions.explanation: The deck flows Stakeholders -> Change Initiation -> Change Request (in the Backlog) -> Change -> New Code plugged into Existing Code to reach Desired Code. The other orderings scramble these steps. (ChangeInitiation p.8)

questions.stem: In the change-initiation flow, the new code is combined with what to reach the desired code?
questions.explanation: New code is plugged into the existing code to reach the desired code, reflecting that change operates on existing code. It is not combined with an empty project, the backlog, or only a test harness. (ChangeInitiation p.8)

questions.stem: The deck's user-story template offers an alternative ending clause. Which is it?
questions.explanation: The alternative template is 'As a [user type], I want [goal] because [why]'. Given/unless/therefore are not the deck's alternative phrasing. (ChangeInitiation p.5)

questions.stem: In the ChangeReqLab, what artifact does the student produce for the portfolio?

options.text: A full UML class diagram of JHotDraw
A user story for a chosen JHotDraw feature

questions.explanation: The ChangeReqLab has the student fork JHotDraw, pick a feature, and write its user story as a portfolio artifact. A full UML diagram, a built JAR, and a benchmark are not the lab deliverable. (ChangeReqLab)

questions.stem: Which workflow does the ChangeReqLab have students adopt for the JHotDraw fork?
questions.explanation: The ChangeReqLab adopts GitHub flow and uses GitHub Projects for the backlog card. Gitflow, trunk-based, and SVN are not the lab's specified workflow. (ChangeReqLab)

questions.stem: A request reads: 'Add credit-card payment, gift-card payment, and refund handling.' Per the deck's rules, what should the maintainer do first?

options.text: Record it as one large user story and proceed
Split it into several user stories because it cannot fit one 3-by-5 card
Reject it because it mixes multiple stakeholders
Convert it into a bug report

questions.explanation: A request bundling several capabilities will not fit one card and must be divided into several user stories. Keeping it as one large story violates the size rule; it is valid new functionality (not a rejection), and it is not a defect (so not a bug report). (ChangeInitiation p.4)

questions.stem: What does concept location accomplish?

options.text: It estimates the cost of a change
It finds the code snippet where a change is to be made
It commits the change to version control

questions.explanation: Concept location finds the code snippet where a change is to be made. Cost estimation, committing, and user-story writing are different activities. (ConceptLocation p.2)

questions.stem: Why is concept location necessary, according to the deck?

options.text: Users never know what they want
Change requests are formulated in domain-concept terms, but those concepts are scattered and renamed inside the code

questions.explanation: Concept location bridges a vocabulary gap: requests use domain concepts, but those concepts are scattered across and renamed inside the code. The other statements are not the deck's stated motivation. (ConceptLocation p.2)

questions.stem: The deck's example request 'Correct error that arises when trying to paste a text' illustrates what about concept location?

options.text: The programmer must locate where 'paste' and 'text' live in the code
The request must be rejected as too vague
Concept location only applies to new features

questions.explanation: The 'paste a text' example shows the programmer must locate where the domain concepts ('paste', 'text') live in the code. The request is actionable, the error is the point, and concept location applies to corrective work too. (ConceptLocation p.2)

questions.stem: According to the deck, what is true about comprehending large programs?

options.text: Large programs cannot be completely comprehended
Only the compiler needs to comprehend the program
Large programs must be fully read before any change

questions.explanation: The deck states large programs cannot be completely comprehended, motivating partial comprehension. Reading everything is infeasible, comprehension is needed, and the point concerns the programmer, not the compiler. (ConceptLocation p.3)

questions.stem: What does the 'as-needed strategy' for code comprehension aim for?

options.text: The minimum essential understanding of how specific concepts are reflected in the code

questions.explanation: The as-needed strategy targets the minimum essential understanding of how specific concepts appear in code. It deliberately avoids whole-system comprehension, full class-diagram memorization, or focusing only on tests. (ConceptLocation p.3)

questions.stem: Which analogy does the deck use for partial code comprehension?

options.text: Building a house brick by brick
Reading a novel cover to cover
Visiting a large city: you learn the route to your destination, not every street

questions.explanation: The deck uses the large-city analogy: you learn the route to your destination, not every street. The other analogies are not used and imply exhaustive or different strategies. (ConceptLocation p.3)

questions.stem: The large-city analogy provides the cognitive justification for which idea?

options.text: Navigating directly to the relevant code snippet instead of reading the whole system
Rewriting the system from scratch when it is too big

questions.explanation: The analogy justifies concept location: navigate to the relevant snippet rather than reading everything. It does not advocate rewrites, pair programming, or shunning documentation. (ConceptLocation p.3)

questions.stem: What are the three corners of the concept triangle?
questions.explanation: The concept triangle has Name, Intension, and Extension. MVC and the OO/IPO triplets are different models, not the concept triangle. (ConceptLocation p.4)

questions.stem: In the concept triangle, what is the Intension of a concept?

options.text: Its definition (its defining properties or meaning)
The set of all its instances

questions.explanation: Intension is the definition or defining properties of the concept. The set of instances is the Extension; the label is the Name; a code location is not a triangle corner. (ConceptLocation p.4)

questions.stem: In the concept triangle, what is the Extension of a concept?

options.text: The natural-language name of the concept
The defining properties of the concept
The regular expression used to find it
The set of all instances the concept denotes

questions.explanation: Extension is the set of all instances the concept denotes. The defining properties are the Intension; the label is the Name; a regex is a search tool, not a triangle corner. (ConceptLocation p.4)

questions.stem: In the concept triangle, what is the Name corner?

options.text: The label or identifier of the concept

questions.explanation: The Name is the label/identifier of the concept. The set of instances is the Extension, defining properties are the Intension, and supplier modules belong to dependency search, not the triangle. (ConceptLocation p.4)

questions.stem: Which edge of the concept triangle is labeled 'naming / definition'?
questions.explanation: The Name-to-Intension edge is naming/definition: a name is given by definition. Name-to-Extension is annotation/traceability; Intension-to-Extension is recognition/location. (ConceptLocation p.4)

questions.stem: Which edge of the concept triangle is labeled 'annotation / traceability'?
questions.explanation: Name-to-Extension is annotation/traceability: names trace to instances. Name-to-Intension is naming/definition; Intension-to-Extension is recognition/location. (ConceptLocation p.4)

questions.stem: Which edge of the concept triangle is labeled 'recognition / location'?
questions.explanation: Intension-to-Extension is recognition/location: from a definition you recognize and locate instances. Name-to-Intension is naming/definition; Name-to-Extension is annotation/traceability. (ConceptLocation p.4)

questions.stem: In the Dog/Pes/Hund triangle example, what do the three words 'Dog', 'Pes', and 'Hund' illustrate?

options.text: Different natural-language names for the same concept

questions.explanation: Dog/Pes/Hund are different natural-language names for the same concept, showing the Name corner can vary. They are not distinct concepts, not instances (Fido, Lassie are), and not the intension. (ConceptLocation p.5)

questions.stem: In the Dog concept example, which is part of the Extension?
questions.explanation: The Extension is the set of instances, e.g. Fido, Lassie, Buck. 'Hairy animal with teeth' is the Intension, 'Hund' is a Name, and recognition is an edge label. (ConceptLocation p.5)

questions.stem: What lesson for concept location does the Dog/Pes/Hund example drive home?

options.text: Names in code are always English
Concepts always have exactly one name
The name a programmer searches for may differ from the name used in code, so reason from intension (meaning) to extension (instances)
Extension and intension are the same thing

questions.explanation: Because names vary, the programmer must reason from intension (meaning) to extension (the actual code instances) rather than relying on a literal name match. Concepts can have many names, intension and extension differ, and code names are not always English. (ConceptLocation p.5)

questions.stem: How does concept location begin when given a change request?

options.text: By reading the request and underlining the domain concepts

questions.explanation: Concept location starts by reading the request and underlining the domain concepts to use as search targets. Compiling, writing tests, or committing are not the starting step. (ConceptLocation p.6)

questions.stem: From the JHotDraw watermark change request, which set of concepts does the deck extract?
questions.explanation: The deck extracts Export, Drawing, Text, Format from the watermark request. The other sets contain plausible but incorrect terms not underlined in that request. (ConceptLocation p.6)

questions.stem: In the watermark request, the deck modifies the 'export' feature to add a watermark text. Why is 'Format' also extracted as a concept?

options.text: Because formatting the source code is required
Because the watermark should be included uniformly for all possible export formats
Because Format is the name of a JHotDraw class
Because the request asks to change the file format

questions.explanation: 'Format' is extracted because the watermark must appear uniformly across all export formats. It is not about source formatting, not a named class, and the request does not change the file format itself. (ConceptLocation p.6)

questions.stem: The watermark change-request slide contains a known typo for the framework name. What is it?
questions.explanation: The slide mistypes JHotDraw as 'JHowDraw'; it is the same framework. The other misspellings are not the documented typo. (ConceptLocation p.6, guide Gotchas)

questions.stem: A maintainer reasons: 'The watermark is text stamped onto an exported drawing,' then looks for classes that actually perform export. Which concept-triangle movement is this?

options.text: From intension (the meaning) to extension (the code instances)

questions.explanation: Reasoning from the meaning ('text stamped onto an export') to the actual exporting classes is the intension-to-extension (recognition/location) movement. The other options misname the direction or use edge labels as movements. (ConceptLocation p.4, Worked Example)

questions.stem: Why can't a maintainer rely on a plain text search for the literal word from a change request to locate a concept?

options.text: The concept's name in code (its Name corner) may differ from the request's wording, so meaning-based recognition (intension to extension) is needed
Every concept has exactly one canonical spelling
Text search tools do not exist for source code
Change requests never contain domain nouns

questions.explanation: Because the Name in code may differ from the request's wording (Dog/Pes/Hund), the maintainer must recognize instances from the intension rather than matching a literal string. Search tools do exist (GREP), spellings vary, and requests do contain domain nouns. (ConceptLocation p.5, p.8)

questions.stem: How many families of concept-location methodologies does the deck describe?
questions.explanation: The deck lists four methodology families: human knowledge, traceability tools, dynamic search, and static search. Three, two, and five miscount. (ConceptLocation p.7)

questions.stem: Which concept-location methodology relies on a programmer who already knows the code pointing to the location?
questions.explanation: Human knowledge uses a programmer's existing familiarity to point to the location. Traceability uses maintained links, dynamic search runs the code, static search analyzes without running. (ConceptLocation p.7)

questions.stem: Which methodology relies on maintained links between requirements/concepts and code?
questions.explanation: Traceability tools maintain links between requirements/concepts and code. Human knowledge is memory-based, dynamic search executes the program, static search inspects code without running. (ConceptLocation p.7)

questions.stem: Which concept-location methodology runs the program and observes which code executes for the concept?
questions.explanation: Dynamic search uses execution traces, running the program to see which code executes; it is the basis of the IDE-debugger lab. Static search does not run code; human knowledge and traceability use memory and links. (ConceptLocation p.7)

questions.stem: Which three techniques fall under the 'static search' methodology?

options.text: Dependency search, pattern matching (GREP), and information retrieval

questions.explanation: Static search comprises dependency search, pattern matching (e.g. GREP), and information retrieval, all without running the code. Execution traces/profiling/breakpoints are dynamic; the other lists are unrelated. (ConceptLocation p.7)

questions.stem: Which property defines 'static search' as opposed to dynamic search?

options.text: It only works on compiled binaries
It locates concepts without running the code

questions.explanation: Static search locates concepts without running the code. Using a debugger to run the program is dynamic search; acceptance tests and binary-only operation are not the defining property. (ConceptLocation p.7)

questions.stem: What does the acronym GREP stand for?
questions.explanation: GREP stands for 'global regular expression print'. The other expansions are invented. (ConceptLocation p.8)

questions.stem: What does GREP do?

options.text: Prints the lines that contain a match for a regular expression
Runs the program and records a trace

questions.explanation: GREP prints lines matching a regular expression. Tracing is dynamic search, building a CDG is dependency search, and renaming is not what GREP does. (ConceptLocation p.8)

questions.stem: How is GREP used as a concept-location technique, per the deck?

options.text: Once: a single exhaustive search finds everything
Iteratively: formulate a query, investigate the results, refine the query
Only after the program is executed
Automatically, with no human in the loop

questions.explanation: GREP is used iteratively: formulate, investigate, refine. It is not a one-shot exhaustive search, not fully automatic, and as a static technique does not require running the program. (ConceptLocation p.8)

questions.stem: What is GREP's main weakness as a concept-location technique?

options.text: It is blind to dependencies and renaming, which dependency search complements

questions.explanation: GREP is fast and tool-free but blind to dependencies and renaming, which is why dependency search complements it. It does search text, does not run the program, and does return matches. (ConceptLocation p.8)

questions.stem: Dependency search is a static method built on what structure extracted from existing code?
questions.explanation: Dependency search uses Class Dependency Graphs extracted from the code. Execution traces drive dynamic search; use-case diagrams and commit graphs are not its basis. (ConceptLocation p.9)

questions.stem: What is a module's 'local functionality'?

options.text: The concepts actually implemented in the module itself, not delegated to others
The functionality described in the user story
All functionality reachable through its suppliers
The functionality of its parent class only

questions.explanation: Local functionality is what the module implements itself, not delegated. Functionality reachable through suppliers is composite; parent-class-only and user-story descriptions are not the definition. (ConceptLocation p.9)

questions.stem: What is a module's 'composite functionality'?

options.text: Its complete functionality combined with all its supporting (supplier) modules
Only the functionality it implements directly

questions.explanation: Composite functionality is the module's complete functionality plus that of all its supplier modules. Direct-only is local functionality; subclasses and contraction are unrelated. (ConceptLocation p.9)

questions.stem: How are local and composite functionality determined, per the deck?
questions.explanation: Both are determined by reading code and documentation. Profilers, LOC counts, and commit counts are not the deck's method. (ConceptLocation p.9)

questions.stem: In the dependency-search algorithm, when do you descend into a module's supplier modules?

options.text: When the concept is absent from both local and composite
When the concept is in the local functionality
When the module has no suppliers
When the concept is in the composite responsibility but NOT in the local functionality

questions.explanation: You descend into suppliers when the concept is in the composite responsibility but not local. If it is local you stop; if absent from both you backtrack. (ConceptLocation p.9-10)

questions.stem: What is the stop condition of the dependency-search algorithm?

options.text: The concept is implemented in the module's local (own) functionality
All suppliers have been marked Next
The CDG has been fully drawn
The concept is anywhere in the composite responsibility

questions.explanation: The search stops when the concept is implemented in the module's local functionality. Composite-only means keep descending; marking suppliers Next or drawing the CDG are not stop conditions. (ConceptLocation p.10, Gotchas)

questions.stem: In the dependency-search algorithm, what do you do when a selected module's composite responsibility does NOT contain the concept?

options.text: Find the set of backtrack modules and back up

questions.explanation: If the composite responsibility lacks the concept, the branch is a dead end, so you find backtrack modules and back up. You only stop when it is local, only descend when composite contains it, and Propagating means composite does contain it. (ConceptLocation p.10)

questions.stem: According to the CLLab, features often start the dependency search from which kind of class?
questions.explanation: The lab notes that features often start from controller classes. Data-access, test, and configuration starting points are not the lab's guidance. (CLLab)

questions.stem: In the interactive Computer/Programmer tool version of the algorithm, who decides whether the concept is implemented in a module?
questions.explanation: The Computer finds candidate module sets; the Programmer judges whether the concept is implemented. The compiler and VCS play no role in this split. (ConceptLocation p.21)

questions.stem: In the interactive tool, what does the Computer side do?

options.text: Decides if the concept is present

questions.explanation: The Computer finds candidate module sets; the Programmer makes the concept judgment. Writing stories and committing are not part of this tool. (ConceptLocation p.21)

questions.stem: What does the 'Blank' search mark mean?

options.text: The class is scheduled for inspection
The class was never inspected and is not scheduled for inspection
The class is a dead end
The class is inspected and contains the concept

questions.explanation: Blank means never inspected and not scheduled. Inspected-and-contains is Propagating, scheduled is Next, dead end is Unchanged. (ConceptLocation p.11)

questions.stem: What does the 'Propagating' search mark mean?

options.text: Inspected; its composite responsibility contains the concept
Inspected; its composite responsibility does not contain the concept

questions.explanation: Propagating means inspected and its composite responsibility contains the concept, so the search propagates into it. Composite-without-concept is Unchanged, never-inspected is Blank, scheduled is Next. (ConceptLocation p.11)

questions.stem: What does the 'Unchanged' search mark mean?

options.text: Inspected; its composite responsibility contains the concept
Inspected; its composite responsibility does not contain the concept (a dead end)

questions.explanation: Unchanged means inspected but the composite responsibility lacks the concept, a dead end for that path. Composite-with-concept is Propagating, scheduled is Next, never-inspected is Blank. (ConceptLocation p.11)

questions.stem: What does the 'Next' search mark mean?

options.text: The class is scheduled for inspection
The class was inspected and is a dead end
The class was inspected and contains the concept
The class is never inspected and not scheduled

questions.explanation: Next means scheduled for inspection. Inspected-with-concept is Propagating, inspected dead end is Unchanged, never-inspected-and-unscheduled is Blank. (ConceptLocation p.11)

questions.stem: What precisely distinguishes the 'Propagating' mark from the 'Unchanged' mark?

options.text: Both mean 'inspected'; the difference is whether the composite responsibility contains the concept
Propagating means scheduled, Unchanged means inspected
Propagating means local match, Unchanged means composite match

questions.explanation: Both Propagating and Unchanged mean inspected; the difference is whether the composite responsibility contains the concept (yes -> Propagating, no -> Unchanged). They are not scheduled/inspected, not local/composite, and not interchangeable. (ConceptLocation p.11, Gotchas)

questions.stem: How does 'Next' differ from 'Blank'?

options.text: Next is inspected; Blank is scheduled
Next means the concept is found; Blank means it is not
Next is scheduled for inspection; Blank is neither inspected nor scheduled
They both mean a dead end

questions.explanation: Next is scheduled for inspection, while Blank is neither inspected nor scheduled. Neither is inspected yet, neither is a dead end (that is Unchanged), and neither indicates the concept is found. (ConceptLocation p.11, Gotchas)

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the four search marks during dependency search?

options.text: To color the final UML diagram
To rank classes by lines of code
To make the search systematic and prevent re-inspecting or losing track of classes

questions.explanation: The marks make the search systematic and stop classes being re-inspected or lost. They are not LOC rankings, work assignments, or diagram styling. (ConceptLocation p.11)

questions.stem: Which ordered set of steps matches the dependency-search activity diagram?

options.text: Find starting modules -> select one -> is concept local? if yes stop -> else is it in composite? if yes find suppliers, if no find backtrack modules
Draw CDG -> mark all Propagating -> stop
Select one -> is concept in composite? if yes stop -> else find suppliers
Find starting modules -> commit -> select one -> stop

questions.explanation: The algorithm finds starting modules, selects one, checks local (stop if yes), then checks composite (descend to suppliers if yes, backtrack if no). The distractors omit the local check, invert the composite stop, or skip the search entirely. (ConceptLocation p.10)

questions.stem: A class is inspected; the concept is not implemented in it directly, but one of its suppliers clearly implements it. How is THIS class marked, and what happens next?

options.text: The search stops at this class because the concept is in its composite
Marked Propagating, and its suppliers are scheduled (Next) so the search descends
Marked Unchanged, and the search backtracks
Marked Blank, and it is ignored

questions.explanation: Because the concept is in the composite (a supplier implements it) but not local, the class is Propagating and its suppliers become Next; the search descends. It is not Unchanged (composite does contain it), not Blank (it was inspected), and the stop condition requires a LOCAL match. (ConceptLocation p.10-11)

questions.stem: In the deck's worked dependency-search trace, which root class is the search rooted at?
questions.explanation: The trace is rooted at UMLEditor (the root highlighted at the start). ClassNode and AbstractNode are where concepts are found; NoteNode is the wrong-way node; RectangularNode is a lower-level node. (ConceptLocation p.12)

questions.stem: In the UMLEditor trace, UMLEditor's direct suppliers are five classes ending in which suffix?
questions.explanation: UMLEditor's five direct suppliers are the *DiagramGraph classes (SequenceDiagramGraph, UseCaseDiagramGraph, ClassDiagramGraph, ObjectDiagramGraph, StateDiagramGraph). Node and Edge are lower-level; Editor is the root. (ConceptLocation p.12-13)

questions.stem: In the UMLEditor trace, which DiagramGraph is chosen as the most likely supplier to follow?
questions.explanation: ClassDiagramGraph is chosen as the most promising supplier (shown with a bold arrow). The other DiagramGraph classes are marked Next but not the one followed first. (ConceptLocation p.14)

questions.stem: In the UMLEditor trace, following toward which class is highlighted as the 'wrong way'?
questions.explanation: Following toward NoteNode is the deliberate 'wrong way'; the concept is not there. ClassNode and AbstractNode are where concepts are found; RectangularNode lies on the valid path. (ConceptLocation p.16)

questions.stem: In the UMLEditor trace, how is NoteNode marked when the search backtracks from it?
questions.explanation: NoteNode is marked Unchanged (grey) because its composite responsibility lacks the concept, and the search backs up. It is not Propagating, not merely scheduled (Next), and not Blank (it was inspected). (ConceptLocation p.17)

questions.stem: In the UMLEditor trace, which class is first identified as the located concept (shown green)?
questions.explanation: ClassNode is the first 'concept location found' (green). NoteNode was the wrong way, UMLEditor is the root, and SequenceDiagramGraph was just a candidate supplier. (ConceptLocation p.18)

questions.stem: After ClassNode, the trace shows a 'possible extension of the search'. Into which classes may it continue?
questions.explanation: The search may continue into RectangularNode/AbstractNode (e.g. to widen the impact), reflecting the RectangularNode-AbstractNode generalization and ClassNode-RectangularNode relation. NoteNode is a dead end; the others are not the extension shown. (ConceptLocation p.19)

questions.stem: In the UMLEditor trace, which class is reached as a further ('another') concept location?
questions.explanation: AbstractNode is reached as another location when the search is extended. NoteNode was a dead end; ObjectDiagramGraph and UMLEditor are not the additional location. (ConceptLocation p.20)

questions.stem: What makes the UMLEditor trace the canonical exam example of dependency search?

options.text: It avoids any mistakes and reaches the concept directly
It uses GREP instead of a CDG
It demonstrates a wrong turn and a backtrack, with the full Next/Propagating/Unchanged marking
It shows only the Initiation phase

questions.explanation: The UMLEditor trace deliberately takes a wrong turn (toward NoteNode), marks it Unchanged, and backtracks, exercising the full marking scheme. It does not avoid mistakes, does not use GREP, and is purely about concept location. (ConceptLocation p.12-20, Gotchas)

questions.stem: In the UMLEditor CDG, the relationship 'ClassNode is a RectangularNode' is what kind of relationship?
questions.explanation: ClassNode is a RectangularNode is a generalization/inheritance relationship (ClassNode specializes RectangularNode, which specializes AbstractNode). Aggregation, message-passing association, and black-diamond composition describe different UML relations. (ConceptLocation p.12)

questions.stem: The change request driving the UMLEditor trace concerns what?
questions.explanation: The UMLEditor trace is driven by a change request about figure properties. The watermark/export/format request is the separate JHotDraw example; undo/redo is not in this trace. (ConceptLocation p.12)

questions.stem: After 'most likely supplier' ClassDiagramGraph is chosen, what happens to its suppliers in the next trace step?

options.text: They are deleted from the CDG
They are committed to version control

questions.explanation: ClassDiagramGraph's suppliers become Next (scheduled). They are not pre-emptively Unchanged, not committed, and not deleted. (ConceptLocation p.15)

questions.stem: Per the deck, backtracking during dependency search should be regarded as what?

options.text: Proof the concept does not exist anywhere
A reason to restart from a different root
An expected, normal part of the search, not a failure
A sign the change request is invalid

questions.explanation: Backtracking is expected and normal (the canonical trace builds it in), not a failure. It does not invalidate the request, force a restart, or prove the concept is absent. (ConceptLocation p.16-17, Gotchas)

questions.stem: The classes identified during concept location are handed off to which next phase as the initial impact set?
questions.explanation: Concept-location classes form the initial impact set consumed by Impact Analysis. Prefactoring, Actualization, and Conclusion come later in the spine. (SoftwareChange p.9)

questions.stem: What is the key distinction between concept location and impact analysis emphasized in the Gotchas?

options.text: Concept location finds the starting snippet/class; impact analysis grows that into the full impact set
They are two names for the same activity
Concept location grows the impact set; impact analysis finds the starting class

questions.explanation: Concept location finds the starting snippet/class (the initial impact set); impact analysis grows it into the full set. The roles are not reversed, not identical, and neither runs in Initiation. (SoftwareChange p.8-9, Gotchas)

questions.stem: In the CLLab, after using the IDE debugger to find classes, what does the student record as a portfolio artifact?

options.text: An initial set of classes in a Domain Class / Responsibility table

questions.explanation: The CLLab deliverable is an initial Domain Class / Responsibility table. A test suite, deployment diagram, and flame graph are not the lab's artifact. (CLLab)

questions.stem: In the CLLab, if the debugger surfaces many classes, which does the student keep?
questions.explanation: When many classes appear, the student keeps only the domain classes for the initial set. Keeping all classes, only tests, or only controllers is not the lab guidance (controllers are merely the starting point). (CLLab)

questions.stem: In the watermark worked example, which methodology does the CLLab approach use to locate the export classes?

options.text: Static GREP over the entire repository
Human knowledge from the original authors
Traceability links maintained in a database
Dynamic search via the IDE debugger, starting from controller classes

questions.explanation: The CLLab approach is dynamic search with the IDE debugger, starting from controller classes (run JHotDraw, trigger export, watch which classes execute). GREP is an optional static seed; human knowledge and traceability are different methodologies. (CLLab, ConceptLocation p.7)

questions.stem: For the watermark request, a maintainer GREPs for 'export', 'write', and 'format' to seed candidate files, then runs the debugger from the export Tool. Which statement best characterizes this combination?

options.text: GREP (static pattern matching) seeds candidates while the debugger (dynamic search) confirms which classes actually execute the export
Neither technique is a concept-location methodology
GREP confirms execution while the debugger only seeds candidates

questions.explanation: GREP is static pattern matching used to seed candidate files; the IDE debugger is dynamic search confirming which classes execute. GREP does not observe execution, the roles are not reversed, and both are concept-location methodologies. (ConceptLocation p.7-8, Worked Example)

questions.stem: Reproducing the UMLEditor trace, you reach a class whose composite responsibility lacks the concept after several descents. What is the correct next action and resulting mark?

options.text: Mark it Propagating and descend into its suppliers
Mark it Unchanged and find backtrack modules to back up
Mark it Next and stop the search
Mark it Blank and re-root at a different DiagramGraph

questions.explanation: A class whose composite lacks the concept is a dead end: mark it Unchanged and backtrack. Propagating/descend applies when composite contains the concept; Next means scheduled (not a stop); Blank is for never-inspected classes. (ConceptLocation p.10-11, p.17)

questions.stem: What is JHotDraw?

options.text: A Java framework for drawing technical and structured graphics

questions.explanation: JHotDraw is a Java framework for drawing technical and structured graphics such as network layouts and Gantt diagrams. It is not a C++ image editor, a database, or a test framework. (JHotDraw p.4)

questions.stem: What kinds of graphics is JHotDraw designed to draw?

options.text: Technical and structured graphics such as network layouts and Gantt diagrams

questions.explanation: JHotDraw targets technical and structured graphics (network layouts, Gantt diagrams). It is not for 3D rendering, photo retouching, or game sprites. (JHotDraw p.4)

questions.stem: JHotDraw was originally developed in which language, and as what?
questions.explanation: It began in Smalltalk as HotDraw before the Java JHotDraw. The other origins are fabricated. (JHotDraw p.4)

questions.stem: Who originally created HotDraw, the Smalltalk predecessor of JHotDraw?
questions.explanation: HotDraw was created by Kent Beck and Ward Cunningham. Gamma/Helm are GoF authors, Fowler wrote on refactoring, and Martin/Johnson are associated with other work. (JHotDraw p.4)

questions.stem: Why is JHotDraw historically significant, per the deck?

options.text: It was the first open-source project on GitHub
It introduced the concept of a compiler
It was one of the first software projects explicitly designed for reuse and labelled a framework
It was the first program written in Java

questions.explanation: JHotDraw was one of the first projects explicitly designed for reuse and labelled a framework. It was not the first Java program, first GitHub project, or related to compiler invention. (JHotDraw p.4)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw's class model, which class is the application/window and extends AWT Frame?
questions.explanation: DrawApplication extends AWT Frame and owns the current Tool and a Drawing. StandardDrawingView extends Panel (the view); Drawing is the figure-container interface; Tool is the controller interface. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: Which JHotDraw class is the view, extends AWT Panel, and holds the selection?
questions.explanation: StandardDrawingView extends AWT Panel, holds the selection, and receives notification from the Drawing. DrawApplication is the window/Frame; Figure and Handle are drawable elements and grips. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw, which interface is the figure container that aggregates Figures?
questions.explanation: Drawing is the figure-container interface aggregating 1..n Figures. Tool is the controller, Handle is an interaction grip, and StandardDrawingView is the view. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw, which interface represents a drawable element that owns Handles and can itself contain figures?
questions.explanation: Figure is the drawable-element interface; it has 1..n Handles and can itself be a figure container (enabling Composite). Drawing is the top-level container, Tool is the controller, DrawApplication is the window. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: Which JHotDraw interface plays the controller role as the application's current tool?
questions.explanation: Tool is the controller interface; the application has one current tool. Figure is a drawable element, Handle is a grip, Drawing is the figure container. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: What do Handles represent in JHotDraw?

options.text: Interaction points on a figure, such as resize or move grips

questions.explanation: Handles are interaction points (resize/move grips) on a figure, with 1..n per Figure. They are not the menu, persistence, or undo history. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: What is the cardinality between DrawApplication and StandardDrawingView?
questions.explanation: DrawApplication is paired 1-to-1 with StandardDrawingView. The other cardinalities do not match the deck's class model. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: What is the cardinality between a Drawing and its Figures?

options.text: A Drawing has exactly one Figure

questions.explanation: A Drawing aggregates 1..n Figures (black-diamond composition). It is not limited to one Figure, the relationship is not inverted, and it holds at least one. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: What is the cardinality between a Figure and its Handles?

options.text: A Figure has at most one Handle
A Figure has exactly zero Handles

questions.explanation: A Figure has 1..n Handles. It is not zero, the relationship is not inverted, and it is not capped at one. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: How do applications reuse the JHotDraw framework at its extension points?

options.text: By subclassing framework interfaces, e.g. MyTool/YourTool extend Tool and MyFigure/YourFigure extend Figure
By editing the framework's source directly
By copying the whole framework into the app
By replacing AWT with a custom GUI toolkit

questions.explanation: Applications subclass framework interfaces (MyTool extends Tool, MyFigure extends Figure) at the extension points. They do not edit framework source, copy it wholesale, or swap the GUI toolkit. (JHotDraw p.7)

questions.stem: The 'frozen spots vs. hot spots' idea in JHotDraw means what?

options.text: Hot spots are bugs to be fixed
The framework's skeleton is fixed (frozen) while you plug in your own tools and figures (hot spots)
The whole framework can be rewritten freely
Only the GUI colors can change

questions.explanation: Frozen spots are the fixed framework skeleton; hot spots are the extension points (your tools and figures). It is not a free rewrite, not just colors, and hot spots are intended extension points, not bugs. (JHotDraw p.7)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw's MVC structure, which elements form the Model?
questions.explanation: The Model is the Figures (with attributes like FillColor, Position) plus the Drawing container. DrawingView/DrawingWindow are the View; Tools are the Controller; Handles are interaction grips. (JHotDraw p.17)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw's MVC structure, which elements form the View?
questions.explanation: The View is DrawingView (a clipping view of a window) and DrawingWindow. Figures/Drawing are the Model, Tools are the Controller, FigureAttributes is model state. (JHotDraw p.17)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw's MVC structure, which elements form the Controller?
questions.explanation: The Controller is the Tools that manipulate the model. Figures/attributes and the Drawing are the Model; DrawingView/Window are the View. (JHotDraw p.17)

questions.stem: Which sequence matches JHotDraw's MVC interaction loop?

options.text: Controller Notify -> Model Update -> View Action
View raises User Action -> Controller -> Update Model -> Model Notify -> View Update
Model raises Action -> View -> Controller Update -> Notify
View Update -> Model Action -> Controller Notify

questions.explanation: The loop is: View raises a User Action, the Controller updates the Model, the Model notifies, and the View updates. The other orderings scramble the MVC roles. (JHotDraw p.17)

questions.stem: A common MVC mistake in JHotDraw is to misplace which element?

options.text: Putting Tools in the model instead of treating them as the Controller

questions.explanation: Tools are the Controller and must not be placed in the model. Figures/Drawing are the Model and DrawingView is the View; the Gotcha specifically warns against putting Tools in the model. (JHotDraw p.17, Gotchas)

questions.stem: In the MVC class-level picture, which class implements the Tool (controller) interface and exposes mouse methods?
questions.explanation: AbstractTool implements the controller interface and exposes mouseDown/Drag/Up/Move plus activate/deactivate. AbstractFigure/StandardDrawing implement model interfaces; FigureAttributes holds attribute state. (JHotDraw p.18)

questions.stem: In the MVC class picture, FigureAttributes holds attribute data in what structure?
questions.explanation: FigureAttributes holds an fMap:Hashtable. It is not a sorted array, a tool list, or a database table. (JHotDraw p.18)

questions.stem: Why is JHotDraw a good case study for a software-maintenance course?

options.text: It has no design patterns at all
It is designed for reuse and pattern-dense, so concept location, impact analysis and pre/postfactoring can be taught cleanly
It is too small to need maintenance
It is deliberately badly written to expose bugs

questions.explanation: JHotDraw is reuse-designed and pattern-dense, making it ideal for teaching change activities cleanly. It is not deliberately bad, not pattern-free, and not trivially small. (JHotDraw p.4, p.16)

questions.stem: The JHotDraw demo GUI shows which sorts of elements?

options.text: Tools, figures (ellipses, rectangles), connections, groups, text, and images

questions.explanation: The demo GUI shows tools, figures (ellipses, rectangles), connections, groups, text, annotations, images, and URL attachments. Spreadsheets, 3D models, and audio are not part of it. (JHotDraw p.4, p.6)

questions.stem: Which statement about JHotDraw's selection and notification is correct?

options.text: DrawApplication holds the selection and notifies the Drawing
The Drawing holds the selection and notifies the Tool
Handles hold the selection and notify the view
StandardDrawingView holds the selection and receives notification from the Drawing

questions.explanation: StandardDrawingView (the view) holds the selection and receives notification from the Drawing (the model). The other options misassign these responsibilities. (JHotDraw p.5)

questions.stem: How many design patterns are in the Gang-of-Four (GoF) catalog?
questions.explanation: The GoF catalog has 23 patterns in three categories. 12, 8, and 50 are not the catalog size (8 is the number JHotDraw emphasizes; 50 is the deck's page count). (JHotDraw p.10, p.16)

questions.stem: Into how many categories by purpose are the 23 GoF patterns divided?
questions.explanation: The 23 patterns fall into three categories by purpose: creational, structural, behavioral. Two, four, and five miscount. (JHotDraw p.10)

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the 'creational' GoF pattern category?

options.text: Initializing and configuring classes and objects
Dealing with dynamic interactions among objects

questions.explanation: Creational patterns initialize/configure classes and objects. Decoupling interface from implementation is structural; dynamic interactions are behavioral; persistence is not a GoF category. (JHotDraw p.10)

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the 'structural' GoF pattern category?
questions.explanation: Structural patterns decouple interface from implementation. Initializing objects is creational; dynamic interactions are behavioral; thread scheduling is not a GoF category. (JHotDraw p.10)

questions.stem: What is the purpose of the 'behavioral' GoF pattern category?

options.text: Dealing with dynamic interactions among objects

questions.explanation: Behavioral patterns deal with dynamic interactions among objects. Initializing is creational; decoupling interface/implementation is structural; compilation is unrelated. (JHotDraw p.10)

questions.stem: Besides purpose, GoF patterns are split by which second dimension?
questions.explanation: The second dimension is scope: Class vs. Object. Language, performance, and age are not GoF classification axes. (JHotDraw p.10-11)

questions.stem: Which is one of the three underlying GoF design principles cited in the deck?

options.text: Program to an interface, not to an implementation

questions.explanation: 'Program to an interface, not to an implementation' is one of the three principles. The others contradict GoF advice (which favors composition over deep inheritance and warns against premature optimization). (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: Which principle does the deck state about composition and inheritance?
questions.explanation: The deck states 'favor composition over class inheritance'. The inverse, banning composition, and multiple inheritance are not the principle. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: Which is the third underlying GoF principle in the deck, about variation?

options.text: Find what varies and encapsulate it
Find what is constant and duplicate it
Find what varies and hard-code it

questions.explanation: The third principle is 'find what varies and encapsulate it'. Duplicating constants, hard-coding variation, and ignoring it are the opposite advice. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: The three underlying GoF principles in the deck collectively aim at what (the slide's Item)?
questions.explanation: The principles aim at flexibility and reuse through decoupling. Speed, memory, and strict type checking are not the stated goal. (JHotDraw p.9)

questions.stem: Which pattern is the ONLY creational pattern with Class scope in the GoF table?
questions.explanation: Factory Method is the class-scoped creational pattern; Abstract Factory, Builder, Prototype, and Singleton are object-scoped creational patterns. (JHotDraw p.12)

questions.stem: Which of these is a creational GoF pattern?
questions.explanation: Singleton is creational. Decorator and Composite are structural; Observer is behavioral. (JHotDraw p.12)

questions.stem: Which of these is a structural GoF pattern?
questions.explanation: Composite is structural. Factory Method is creational; Strategy and Memento are behavioral. (JHotDraw p.13)

questions.stem: Which of these is a behavioral GoF pattern?
questions.explanation: Strategy is behavioral. Adapter and Proxy are structural; Builder is creational. (JHotDraw p.14)

questions.stem: Which GoF pattern is listed with both Class and Object scope (the Adapter variants)?
questions.explanation: Adapter appears in both class and object forms. Bridge, Facade, and Flyweight are object-scoped structural patterns. (JHotDraw p.13)

questions.stem: Which two behavioral patterns are Class-scoped in the GoF table?
questions.explanation: Interpreter and Template Method are the class-scoped behavioral patterns; the rest (Observer, Strategy, Command, Iterator, State, Visitor, etc.) are object-scoped. (JHotDraw p.14)

questions.stem: What is the deck's single-sentence thesis linking design patterns to maintainability?

options.text: Extensibility of frameworks calls for extensive use of design patterns
Frameworks should avoid patterns to stay simple

questions.explanation: The thesis is that the extensibility of frameworks calls for extensive use of design patterns. The other statements contradict the deck. (JHotDraw p.16)

questions.stem: Who are the 'Gang of Four' behind the GoF patterns?
questions.explanation: The Gang of Four are Gamma, Helm, Johnson, and Vlissides (GHJV). The other groups are notable but not the GoF authors. (JHotDraw p.10, p.16)

questions.stem: Which list correctly assigns three patterns to their categories?

options.text: Singleton structural, Decorator behavioral, Observer creational
Singleton creational, Decorator behavioral, Observer structural
Singleton creational, Decorator structural, Observer behavioral
Singleton behavioral, Decorator creational, Observer structural

questions.explanation: Singleton is creational, Decorator is structural, Observer is behavioral. The other rows scramble at least one assignment. (JHotDraw p.12-14)

questions.stem: Why does a reuse-oriented framework like JHotDraw end up pattern-dense, per the deck's reasoning?

options.text: Because patterns are required by the Java language
Because patterns reduce the number of classes to one
