Because frameworks cannot compile without patterns
Because extensibility demands decoupling variation from the fixed skeleton, which is exactly what design patterns provide

questions.explanation: Extensibility requires decoupling what varies from the frozen skeleton, and design patterns are the established way to achieve that, so frameworks become pattern-dense. Patterns are not a language requirement, do not collapse to one class, and are not needed for compilation. (JHotDraw p.9, p.16)

questions.stem: Builder, Prototype, and Abstract Factory all belong to which GoF category?
questions.explanation: Builder, Prototype, and Abstract Factory are all creational (object-scoped). They are not structural or behavioral, and the three categories do exist. (JHotDraw p.12)

questions.stem: Bridge, Facade, and Proxy all belong to which GoF category?
questions.explanation: Bridge, Facade, and Proxy are structural patterns. They are not creational or behavioral, and 'concurrency' is not a GoF category. (JHotDraw p.13)

questions.stem: Chain of Responsibility, Mediator, and Visitor all belong to which GoF category?
questions.explanation: Chain of Responsibility, Mediator, and Visitor are behavioral. They are not creational or structural, and 'architectural' is not a GoF category. (JHotDraw p.14)

questions.stem: Which set of patterns does the deck say JHotDraw makes extensive use of?

options.text: Observer, Mediator, Visitor, Chain of Responsibility, Command, Iterator, Facade, Abstract Factory
Singleton, Proxy, Flyweight, Memento, Interpreter, Bridge, Builder, Adapter
MVC, Composite, Strategy, State, Template Method, Decorator, Factory Method, Prototype

questions.explanation: JHotDraw uses MVC, Composite, Strategy, State, Template Method, Decorator, Factory Method, and Prototype. The other sets list patterns not emphasized for JHotDraw. (JHotDraw p.16)

questions.stem: What is the intent of the Composite pattern?

options.text: Compose objects into tree structures so clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly
Define a family of interchangeable algorithms
Attach responsibilities to an object dynamically
Let an object change behavior when its state changes

questions.explanation: Composite composes objects into part-whole trees, treating leaves and composites uniformly. Dynamic responsibilities is Decorator; interchangeable algorithms is Strategy; state-driven behavior is State. (JHotDraw p.19)

questions.stem: How is Composite used in JHotDraw?

options.text: Each tool clones a prototype figure
A figure is composed of several figures, so scale, rotation, and move act uniformly on all
Borders and shadows are attached to a figure
Layout algorithms are separated from the figures

questions.explanation: In JHotDraw a figure can be composed of several figures, treated uniformly so operations like scale/rotation/move apply to all. Cloning is Prototype, separated layouts is Strategy, borders/shadows is Decorator. (JHotDraw p.22)

questions.stem: Which JHotDraw class delegates its methods to all contained shapes, embodying Composite?
questions.explanation: GraphicalCompositeFigure delegates methods to all contained shapes (nested to arbitrary depth). RectangleFigure is a leaf, LineConnection is Template Method, BorderDecorator is Decorator. (JHotDraw p.23)

questions.stem: What is the documented 'type-safety trap' of Composite in JHotDraw?

options.text: instanceof always returns the wrong type
A composite can hold only one child
You cannot use the type system to restrict which components a composite holds, so run-time checks are needed

questions.explanation: Composite cannot use the type system to restrict a composite's components, so run-time checks are required. The instanceof issue is the Decorator trap; composites can nest arbitrarily and hold many children. (JHotDraw p.24, Gotchas)

questions.stem: A documented 'pro' of Composite is what?

options.text: Client code accepting a primitive also accepts a composite, keeping clients simpler
It eliminates the need for any base component type
It guarantees compile-time type restriction of children

questions.explanation: A pro is that client code accepting a primitive also accepts a composite, simplifying clients and easing addition of new component kinds. Compile-time restriction is impossible (the con), a Component type is still needed, and run-time checks are still required. (JHotDraw p.24)

questions.stem: What is the intent of the Strategy pattern?

options.text: Define a family of algorithms, encapsulate each, and make them interchangeable
Create objects by cloning a prototype

questions.explanation: Strategy defines a family of algorithms, encapsulates each, and makes them interchangeable so the algorithm varies independently of clients. Trees is Composite, dynamic responsibilities is Decorator, cloning is Prototype. (JHotDraw p.25)

questions.stem: How is Strategy used in JHotDraw?

options.text: Menus and tools are created by factory methods
Layout algorithms are separated from the objects being laid out, mirroring AWT/Swing layout managers

questions.explanation: Strategy separates layout algorithms (layouters) from the laid-out objects, like AWT/Swing layout managers. Tool modes is State, figures-of-figures is Composite, menus/tools is Factory Method. (JHotDraw p.28)

questions.stem: In the Strategy example, what can a 'layouter' be attached to in JHotDraw?

options.text: A single Handle, to resize it
A composite figure, to render it differently (no layout, align-to-top, align-to-left)

questions.explanation: A layouter attaches to a composite figure to arrange it (no layout, align-to-top, align-to-left). It is not attached to a Handle, the window title, or a commit. (JHotDraw p.28)

questions.stem: A documented 'con' of Strategy is what?

options.text: Clients must know the strategies, and there is communication overhead and more objects
It removes the ability to choose implementations
It hard-codes the algorithm into the context

questions.explanation: Strategy's cons include that clients must know the strategies, Strategy-Context communication overhead, and more objects. It actually enables algorithm families and implementation choice and decouples (not hard-codes) the algorithm. (JHotDraw p.29)

questions.stem: What is the intent of the State pattern?

options.text: Allow an object to alter its behavior when its internal state changes, so it appears to change its class

questions.explanation: State lets an object change behavior with its internal state, appearing to change class. Interchangeable algorithms is Strategy, cloning is Prototype, dynamic responsibilities is Decorator. (JHotDraw p.30)

questions.stem: What is the goal of using State in JHotDraw?

options.text: To externalize a tool's state so a tool can operate in different modes

questions.explanation: State externalizes a tool's state so it can operate in different modes (e.g. zoom in/out, select border/text). Layout separation is Strategy, composition is Composite, cloning is Prototype. (JHotDraw p.32)

questions.stem: In the State example, a SelectTool holds states ZeroClickState, OneClickState, TwoClickState. In a text field, what does the deck say 2 clicks do?
questions.explanation: The deck's click states: 1 click sets the cursor, 2 clicks select a word, 3 clicks select the whole line. Setting the cursor is 1 click; selecting the line is 3 clicks; deselecting is not in the example. (JHotDraw p.32)

questions.stem: A documented 'pro' of State is what?

options.text: It localizes state-specific behavior and makes transitions explicit, with new states added as subclasses
It hides the existence of states from the design
It guarantees fewer total objects than a conditional

questions.explanation: State localizes and partitions state-specific behavior, makes transitions explicit, and lets new states/transitions be added as subclasses (states can be shared). It does not minimize object count, hide states, or remove the Context. (JHotDraw p.33)

questions.stem: What is the intent of the Template Method pattern?

options.text: Define the skeleton of an algorithm, deferring some steps to subclasses

questions.explanation: Template Method defines an algorithm's skeleton and defers some steps to subclasses without changing the structure. Dynamic responsibilities is Decorator, trees is Composite, cloning is Prototype. (JHotDraw p.34)

questions.stem: Which JHotDraw class embodies Template Method for connecting figures with a line?
questions.explanation: LineConnection holds the invariant 'connect figures via a line' algorithm and exposes variant steps as overridable methods. GraphicalCompositeFigure is Composite, DecoratorFigure is Decorator, StandardDrawingView is the view. (JHotDraw p.35)

questions.stem: In the Template Method LineConnection example, what is invariant and what varies?

options.text: The connection semantics are invariant; the line drawing varies
Everything is invariant; subclasses cannot change anything
Nothing is invariant; subclasses rewrite the whole method
The 'connecting figures with a line' algorithm is invariant; the connection semantics (e.g. Petri-net vs. class-diagram) vary in overridden steps

questions.explanation: From the framework's view, connecting figures with a line is always the same (invariant); the connection semantics (Petri-net vs. class-diagram) vary via overridden hook steps. The other options invert or deny the invariant/variant split. (JHotDraw p.35)

questions.stem: What is the intent of the Decorator pattern?

options.text: Attach additional responsibilities to an object dynamically, as a flexible alternative to subclassing
Define a family of interchangeable algorithms
Specify objects to create via a prototypical instance

questions.explanation: Decorator attaches responsibilities dynamically, a flexible alternative to subclassing. Interchangeable algorithms is Strategy, trees is Composite, prototypical creation is Prototype. (JHotDraw p.36)

questions.stem: How is Decorator used in JHotDraw?

options.text: To add specific visualization (borders and shadows) to a generic figure via DecoratorFigure
To separate layout algorithms from figures

questions.explanation: Decorator adds specific visualization (borders and shadows) to a generic figure using DecoratorFigure with BorderDecorator and ShadowDecorator. Cloning is Prototype, layout separation is Strategy, menus/tools is Factory Method. (JHotDraw p.39)

questions.stem: What is the documented 'type-identity trap' of Decorator in JHotDraw?

options.text: A decorator can wrap only one component
A decorated figure cannot be drawn
Java's instanceof won't see the wrapped figure's true type unless exposed via something like getDecoratedType()

questions.explanation: With Decorator, instanceof won't see through the wrapper unless the type is exposed (e.g. getDecoratedType()); a decorator and its component aren't identical. Decorators can wrap, add state, and be drawn. (JHotDraw p.39-40, Gotchas)

questions.stem: A documented 'con' of Decorator is what?

options.text: It cannot add responsibilities at run-time
It is less flexible than static inheritance
A decorator and its component aren't identical, and there are lots of little objects

questions.explanation: Decorator's cons are that a decorator and its component aren't identical and there are many little objects. It actually adds responsibilities dynamically, avoids subclass explosion, and is more flexible than static inheritance. (JHotDraw p.40)

questions.stem: What is the intent of the Factory Method pattern?

options.text: Define an interface for creating an object but let subclasses decide which class to instantiate

questions.explanation: Factory Method defines a creation interface and lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate. Dynamic responsibilities is Decorator, trees is Composite, externalizing state is State. (JHotDraw p.41)

questions.stem: How is Factory Method used in JHotDraw?

options.text: Borders and shadows decorate a figure
DrawApplication defines createMenus() and createTools(), which customized subclasses override to add tools
Each tool clones a prototype figure

questions.explanation: Factory Method keeps menus and tools flexible: DrawApplication defines createMenus()/createTools() that subclasses like ClassDiagrammDrawApplication override. Cloning is Prototype, decoration is Decorator, layout is Strategy. (JHotDraw p.43)

questions.stem: A documented consequence of Factory Method in JHotDraw is what?

options.text: It eliminates the need for any subclasses
It provides hooks for subclasses and connects parallel class hierarchies

questions.explanation: Factory Method provides subclass hooks and connects parallel class hierarchies. It relies on subclasses (not eliminates them), enables adding tools, and keeps menus flexible rather than hard-coded. (JHotDraw p.44)

questions.stem: What is the intent of the Prototype pattern?

options.text: Define an algorithm skeleton with deferred steps
Specify objects to create using a prototypical instance, creating new objects by cloning it

questions.explanation: Prototype specifies objects to create via a prototypical instance and clones it. Interchangeable algorithms is Strategy, dynamic responsibilities is Decorator, algorithm skeletons is Template Method. (JHotDraw p.45)

questions.stem: How is Prototype used in JHotDraw?

options.text: Each tool is initialized with a prototype figure and clones it when creating a new figure
Each composite figure delegates to its children
Each figure is decorated with borders and shadows
Each tool overrides createTools() to add menus

questions.explanation: In JHotDraw each tool holds a prototype figure and clones it (prototype.clone()) to create new figures (Circle Tool clones a circle, Rectangle Tool clones a rectangle). The others describe Factory Method, Decorator, and Composite. (JHotDraw p.46-47)

questions.stem: A documented consequence of Prototype in JHotDraw is what?

options.text: It requires a parallel factory class hierarchy
It hides concrete product classes from clients and lets products be added or removed at run-time
It forces clients to know every concrete class name

questions.explanation: Prototype hides concrete product classes (fewer names to know) and lets products be added/removed at run-time. It avoids a parallel factory hierarchy, relies on cloning, and reduces (not increases) the names clients must know. (JHotDraw p.48)

questions.stem: To add a NEW figure type to JHotDraw with minimal impact, which combination of patterns does the guide describe?

options.text: Add a State subclass and a new Strategy layouter
Wrap it in a Decorator and override LineConnection
Subclass Figure (Composite hierarchy), register a Prototype so a tool can clone it, and let Factory Methods wire it into menus/tools

questions.explanation: Adding a figure type uses the Composite hierarchy (subclass Figure), a Prototype for cloning, and Factory Methods to wire menus/tools. State/Strategy add tool modes/layouts, Decorator/Template Method add embellishments/connections, and Singleton/Observer are not the mechanism. (JHotDraw p.23, p.43, p.47)

questions.stem: To add a NEW tool mode to JHotDraw with low impact, what does the guide recommend?

options.text: Add a State subclass rather than editing conditionals

questions.explanation: A new tool mode is added as a State subclass instead of editing large conditionals. A Strategy adds a layout, a Decorator adds visual embellishment, and a Factory Method wires menus/tools. (JHotDraw p.32, JHotDraw Connection)

questions.stem: To add a NEW layout to JHotDraw without touching figures, what does the guide recommend?
questions.explanation: A new layout is added as a Strategy (layouter) without touching figures. State adds a tool mode, Decorator adds embellishment, and overriding LineConnection adds a connection semantics. (JHotDraw p.28, JHotDraw Connection)

questions.stem: To support a NEW connection semantics (e.g. a Petri-net link) in JHotDraw, what does the guide recommend?

options.text: Wrap the figure in a Decorator
Override the variant hooks of the Template Method LineConnection

questions.explanation: A new connection semantics is supported by overriding the variant hooks of the Template Method LineConnection. Prototypes add figure types, Strategy adds layouts, Decorator adds embellishments. (JHotDraw p.35, JHotDraw Connection)

questions.stem: The watermark feature is most plausibly designed (per the worked example) using which pattern(s)?

options.text: A Strategy layouter applied to the canvas
A Singleton holding the watermark text
A Decorator on figures or a step in each format writer via Template Method
A State machine on the export menu

questions.explanation: The worked example suggests the watermark is likely a Decorator on figures or a step in each format writer via Template Method. A Singleton, Strategy layouter, or State machine are not the suggested designs. (Worked Example, JHotDraw p.35, p.39)

questions.stem: Why does the guide call JHotDraw's pattern-based changes 'low-impact'?

options.text: Because patterns delete old code automatically
Each change plugs into a designed extension point (subclass/clone/strategy), matching prefactoring's goal of minimizing impact

questions.explanation: Pattern-based changes are low-impact because they plug into designed extension points, aligning with prefactoring's minimize-impact goal. Compile speed, automatic deletion, and dependency-freeness are not the reason. (JHotDraw Connection, SoftwareChange p.10)

questions.stem: In the Composite structure, which operations does the Component declare?
questions.explanation: The Composite Component declares Operation plus Add/Remove/GetChild; the Composite forwards Operation() to each child. clone/draw belong to Prototype/Figure, mouse methods to Tool, create* to Factory Method. (JHotDraw p.20-21)

questions.stem: In the Strategy structure, what does the Context hold?

options.text: A reference to a Strategy whose AlgorithmInterface() it uses

questions.explanation: The Strategy Context holds a Strategy and calls its AlgorithmInterface(); ConcreteStrategyA/B/C implement it. A figure list, a prototype clone, and handles belong to other patterns. (JHotDraw p.27)

questions.stem: In the State structure, how does the Context handle a Request()?
questions.explanation: The State Context delegates Request() to state.Handle(), where ConcreteStateA/B implement Handle(). Cloning, child iteration, and factory calls belong to Prototype, Composite, and Factory Method. (JHotDraw p.31)

questions.stem: In the Decorator structure, how does the Decorator implement Operation()?

options.text: It forwards to component.Operation(), optionally adding behavior

questions.explanation: The Decorator holds a component and forwards Operation() to it (component->Operation()), with ConcreteDecorators adding state/behavior. Returning a product is Factory Method, child iteration is Composite, state.Handle() is State. (JHotDraw p.38)

questions.stem: In the Factory Method structure, what does ConcreteCreator override the factory method to do?
questions.explanation: The ConcreteCreator overrides FactoryMethod() to return new ConcreteProduct(), used by AnOperation(). The other behaviors belong to Decorator, Prototype, and State. (JHotDraw p.42)

questions.stem: When should you use Strategy, per the deck's 'use when' list?

options.text: When you must add responsibilities to single objects transparently
When many classes differ only in behavior, or a class has many behaviors as conditionals
When an object must change behavior as its internal state changes
When you must compose part-whole hierarchies

questions.explanation: Strategy fits when classes differ only in behavior, you need algorithm variants, an algorithm uses hidden data, or a class has many conditional behaviors. State-driven behavior is State, part-whole is Composite, transparent responsibilities is Decorator. (JHotDraw p.26)

questions.stem: When should you use State, per the deck's 'use when' list?

options.text: When many classes differ only by interchangeable algorithm
When you must clone prototypes at run-time
When behavior depends on state and must change at run-time, or operations have large multipart state-dependent conditionals
When subclassing would cause a subclass explosion of responsibilities

questions.explanation: State fits when behavior depends on run-time state or operations have big state-dependent conditionals. Interchangeable algorithms is Strategy, cloning is Prototype, avoiding subclass explosion of responsibilities is Decorator. (JHotDraw p.30)

questions.stem: When should you use Decorator, per the deck's 'use when' list?

options.text: When you need a family of interchangeable algorithms
When you must compose objects into trees
When an object must appear to change its class with state
When adding responsibilities to individual objects transparently, for responsibilities that can be withdrawn, or to avoid a subclass explosion

questions.explanation: Decorator fits for transparent, withdrawable per-object responsibilities and to avoid subclass explosion. Algorithm families is Strategy, trees is Composite, state-driven class change is State. (JHotDraw p.37)

questions.stem: When should you use Prototype, per the deck's 'use when' list?

options.text: When classes to instantiate are specified at run-time, to avoid a parallel factory hierarchy, or when instances have few state combinations
When you must define an algorithm skeleton
When you must compose part-whole hierarchies
When behavior depends on internal state

questions.explanation: Prototype fits when classes are specified at run-time (dynamic loading), to avoid a parallel factory hierarchy, or when there are few state combinations (cloning beats manual instantiation). The others describe State, Composite, and Template Method. (JHotDraw p.45-46)

questions.stem: When should you use Factory Method, per the deck's 'use when' list?

options.text: When behavior must change with run-time state
When a class can't anticipate the class of objects it must create, or wants subclasses to specify created objects
When you must attach withdrawable responsibilities
When you need to clone an existing instance

questions.explanation: Factory Method fits when a class cannot anticipate which class to instantiate, wants subclasses to choose, or to localize the delegate helper subclass. Cloning is Prototype, withdrawable responsibilities is Decorator, run-time state is State. (JHotDraw p.41)

questions.stem: A tool must behave differently in 'zoom in' versus 'zoom out' modes, switching at run-time. Which pattern fits, and why not Strategy?

options.text: Prototype, because each mode clones a figure
Decorator, because zoom adds a withdrawable responsibility
State, because the variation is driven by the object's changing internal mode rather than a client-selected algorithm
Strategy, because zoom is just an interchangeable algorithm with no state

questions.explanation: State fits because the tool's behavior changes with its internal mode at run-time (the object appears to change class). Strategy is about client-selected interchangeable algorithms, not internal state transitions; zoom is not a withdrawable decoration or a clone. (JHotDraw p.30, p.32)

questions.stem: Strategy and State share an almost identical class structure (a Context delegating to a swappable object). What conceptual difference does the deck draw between them?

options.text: Strategy is creational while State is structural
Strategy clones prototypes while State decorates objects
Strategy composes trees while State defines algorithm skeletons
Strategy varies an interchangeable algorithm chosen externally; State varies behavior driven by the object's internal state transitions

questions.explanation: Both have a Context delegating to a swappable object, but Strategy encapsulates interchangeable algorithms chosen externally, while State encapsulates state-driven behavior with explicit transitions. Both are behavioral (not creational/structural), and neither is about cloning, decorating, trees, or skeletons. (JHotDraw p.25-33)

questions.stem: Composite and Decorator both wrap a component and forward operations. What is the deck's key structural difference?

options.text: Composite forwards an operation to MANY children (part-whole tree), while a Decorator forwards to ONE wrapped component and adds behavior
Composite adds behavior while Decorator builds trees
Composite is creational while Decorator is behavioral
They are identical patterns with different names

questions.explanation: Composite forwards Operation() to all children to model part-whole trees, while a Decorator wraps a single component and adds responsibilities. Both are structural (not creational/behavioral) and are distinct patterns. (JHotDraw p.20, p.38)

questions.stem: You start dependency search at a controller, find the concept is in the composite (not local), descend through two suppliers, then hit a class whose composite lacks the concept, and finally find a supplier whose LOCAL functionality implements it. What is the correct final marking sequence for these three classes (controller, dead-end, target)?

options.text: Controller: Propagating; dead-end: Unchanged; target: located (stop)
Controller: Next; dead-end: Blank; target: Propagating
Controller: Unchanged; dead-end: Propagating; target: Next

questions.explanation: The controller's composite contains the concept, so Propagating; the dead-end's composite lacks it, so Unchanged; the target locally implements it, so the search stops there (located). The other sequences misapply the marks or the stop condition. (ConceptLocation p.10-11)

questions.stem: What is the correct phase order for these activities: (i) commit and build new baseline, (ii) extract Export/Drawing/Text/Format, (iii) determine the impact set, (iv) write 'As a trial user, I want...'?
questions.explanation: Writing the user story is Initiation (iv), extracting concepts is Concept Location (ii), determining the impact set is Impact Analysis (iii), and committing/building the baseline is Conclusion (i): order (iv),(ii),(iii),(i). The other orders misplace at least one phase. (SoftwareChange p.5-14)

questions.stem: Which statement about the MVC class-level interfaces in JHotDraw is correct?

options.text: Handle is the <<model>> interface and Drawing is the <<view>> interface
Tool is the <<model>> interface and Figure is the <<controller>> interface
DrawingView is the <<controller>> interface and Drawing is the <<view>> interface
Figure and Drawing are <<model>> interfaces, DrawingView is the <<view>> interface, and Tool is the <<controller>> interface

questions.explanation: Figure/Drawing are model interfaces (AbstractFigure, StandardDrawing), DrawingView is the view interface (StandardDrawingView), and Tool is the controller interface (AbstractTool). The other options swap the MVC roles. (JHotDraw p.18)

questions.stem: Why are pre- and post-factoring BOTH considered refactoring under the strict definition?

options.text: Both change structure without changing behavior, only differing in timing relative to the change
Prefactoring changes behavior but postfactoring does not
Both add new behavior to the system

questions.explanation: Refactoring is strictly changing structure without changing behavior; prefactoring (before, to minimize impact) and postfactoring (after, to remove anti-patterns) both satisfy this, differing only in timing. They do not add behavior, both preserve behavior, and both alter code. (SoftwareChange p.4, p.10, p.12, Gotchas)

questions.stem: A teammate claims 'Verification is the last phase, run once after Conclusion.' Why is this wrong?

options.text: Verification only happens during Concept Location
Verification runs vertically alongside Prefactoring through Conclusion, not as a single final step
Verification is actually the first phase, before Initiation

questions.explanation: Verification spans the implementation phases (Prefactoring through Conclusion), guaranteeing correctness throughout; it is not a single post-Conclusion step. It is not first, not confined to Concept Location, and not the same as Conclusion. (SoftwareChange p.5, p.13, Gotchas)

questions.stem: A maintainer stops dependency search the moment a class's COMPOSITE responsibility contains the concept. What rule did they violate?

options.text: Composite responsibility is irrelevant to the search
They should never stop the search at all
The stop condition requires the concept in the LOCAL functionality, not merely the composite
They should have stopped even earlier, at the first Next mark

questions.explanation: The stop condition is a LOCAL match; a composite match only means descend into suppliers. Stopping at the first Next mark or never stopping are both wrong, and composite responsibility is central (it decides descent vs. backtrack). (ConceptLocation p.10, Gotchas)

questions.stem: After page 48, the JHotDraw deck has an 'Other Design Patterns' divider on pages 49-50. What content follows it?

options.text: A full UML model of the Observer pattern
Detailed coverage of Singleton and Proxy
A performance comparison of all patterns
No further content (it is only a divider)

questions.explanation: Pages 49-50 are an 'Other Design Patterns' divider with no further content. There is no detailed Singleton/Proxy, Observer model, or performance comparison after it. (JHotDraw p.49-50, Source Map)

questions.stem: In JHotDraw's Factory Method example, which methods does DrawApplication expose for subclasses to override?
questions.explanation: DrawApplication defines createMenus() and createTools() for subclasses to override. clone/draw, mouse/activate, and Add/GetChild belong to Prototype/Figure, Tool, and Composite. (JHotDraw p.43)

questions.stem: Which concrete decorators does JHotDraw provide via DecoratorFigure?
questions.explanation: DecoratorFigure provides BorderDecorator and ShadowDecorator. CircleTool/RectangleTool relate to Prototype, the click states to State, and the DiagramGraph classes to the UMLEditor trace. (JHotDraw p.39)

questions.stem: Which is the correct ordering of the methodology families from 'relies on a person's memory' to 'analyzes code without running it'?

options.text: Dynamic search, then static search, then human knowledge, then traceability
Static search, then dynamic search, then traceability, then human knowledge
Human knowledge, then traceability tools, then dynamic search, then static search
Traceability, then human knowledge, then static, then dynamic

questions.explanation: The deck lists them as human knowledge, traceability tools, dynamic search (runs the code), and static search (analyzes without running). The other orderings do not match the deck's list. (ConceptLocation p.7)

questions.stem: Which artifact does Concept Location feed forward, and which phase consumes it?

options.text: The user story, consumed by Initiation
The new baseline, consumed by Conclusion
The initial set of located classes, consumed by Impact Analysis

questions.explanation: Concept Location produces the initial set of located classes consumed by Impact Analysis. The user story is an Initiation input, the baseline is a Conclusion output, and the layouter is a Strategy artifact. (SoftwareChange p.8-9)
